{{Short description|Men's national association football team}}

{{Use British English|date = June 2024}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=June 2024}}

| pattern_ra1        = _nir26h

| rightarm1          = 008000
| shorts1            = 22913C
| socks1             = 1F504C

| pattern_sh2        = _nir26a

| shorts2            = FFFFFF
| socks2             = 36655b
| First game         = '''{{fb|IRE}}''' 0–13 {{fb-rt|ENG}}<br />([[Belfast]], Ireland; 18 February 1882)<br />''as '''Northern Ireland'''''{{refn|group=note|The team did not use the team name Northern Ireland until 1952, when the name was used in a one-off friendly against France. The '[[Ulster Banner]]', the flag currently used by the Northern Ireland team, did not become the official flag of [[Northern Ireland]] until 1953. However, the team's first appearance after the end of Ireland (IFA) was against England in October 1950 in the [[1950–51 British Home Championship]].}}<br />{{fb|NIR|name=Ireland|1924}} 1–4 {{fb-rt|ENG}}<br />([[Belfast]], Northern Ireland; 7 October 1950)<hr />{{fb|FRA|1830}} 3–1 {{fb-rt|NIR}}<br />([[Colombes]], France; 11 November 1952)
| Largest win        = '''{{fb|IRE}}''' 7–0 {{fb-rt|WAL|1807}}<br />([[Belfast]], Ireland; 1 February 1930)<br />''as '''Northern Ireland'''''<br />{{fb|NIR}} 5–0 {{fb-rt|CYP}}<br />([[Belfast]], Northern Ireland; 21 April 1971)<br />{{nowrap|{{fb|FRO}} 0–5 {{fb-rt|NIR}}}}<br />([[Landskrona]], Sweden; 11 September 1991)<br />{{nowrap|{{fb|NIR}} 5–0 {{fb-rt|BUL}}}}<br />([[Belfast]], Northern Ireland; 15 October 2024)
| Largest loss       = '''{{fb|IRE}}''' 0–13 {{fb-rt|ENG}}<br />([[Belfast]], Ireland; 18 February 1882)<br />''as '''Northern Ireland'''''<br />{{fb|NED}} 6–0 {{fb-rt|NIR}}<br />([[Amsterdam]], Netherlands; 2 June 2012)
| World cup apps     = 3
| World cup first    = 1958
| World cup best     = Quarter-finals ([[1958 FIFA World Cup|1958]])
| Regional name      = [[UEFA European Championship|European Championship]]
| Regional cup apps  = 1
| Regional cup first = [[UEFA Euro 2016|2016]]
| Regional cup best  = Round of 16 ([[UEFA Euro 2016|2016]])
}}
[[File:Ulster Banner.svg|thumb|[[Ulster Banner]], the flag of the Northern Ireland national football team]]
The '''Northern Ireland national football team''' (also known as the Green and White Army - GAWA) represents [[Northern Ireland]] in men's international [[association football]]. From 1882 to 1950, all of [[Ireland]] was represented by a single side, the [[Ireland national football team (1882–1950)|Ireland national football team]], organised by the [[Irish Football Association]] (IFA). In 1921, the jurisdiction of the IFA was reduced to Northern Ireland following the secession of clubs in the soon-to-be [[Irish Free State]], although its national team included players from all of Ireland until 1950, and used the name ''Ireland'' until the 1970s.<ref>Matthew Taylor (2008). ''The Association Game: A History of British Football''. Harlow:Pearson Education Ltd.</ref>{{refn|group=note|The last match played as Ireland was in 1978 against Scotland.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://nifootball.blogspot.co.uk/2010/03/northern-ireland-programmes-1975-1978_27.html|title=NIFG: Northern Ireland Programmes 1975–1978|access-date=13 June 2016|archive-date=4 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140304095940/http://nifootball.blogspot.co.uk/2010/03/northern-ireland-programmes-1975-1978_27.html|url-status=live}}</ref> However, apart from this match, all British Championship matches had been played as "Northern Ireland" since the 1973–74 tournament.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://nifootball.blogspot.co.uk/2010/03/northern-ireland-programmes-1972-1975.html|title=NIFG: Northern Ireland Programmes 1972–1975|access-date=13 June 2016|archive-date=4 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140304093931/http://nifootball.blogspot.co.uk/2010/03/northern-ireland-programmes-1972-1975.html|url-status=live}}</ref> In the 1972–73 tournament, the first two matches were played as "Ireland" and the third as "Northern Ireland". In the 1971–72 tournament, the first was played as "Ireland" and the second and third as "Northern Ireland". 1970–71 was the last tournament in which all matches were played under the name "Ireland".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://nifootball.blogspot.co.uk/2010/03/northern-ireland-programmes-1882-1889.html|title=NIFG: Northern Ireland Programmes 1968–1972|access-date=13 June 2016|archive-date=15 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170815174654/http://nifootball.blogspot.co.uk/2010/03/northern-ireland-programmes-1882-1889.html|url-status=live}}</ref>}} The [[Football Association of Ireland]] (FAI) organises the separate [[Republic of Ireland national football team]].

Although part of the [[United Kingdom]], Northern Ireland has always had a representative side that plays in major professional tournaments&nbsp;– whether alongside the rest of Ireland pre-1922 or as its own entity&nbsp;– though not in the Olympic Games, as the [[International Olympic Committee]] (IOC) has always recognised United Kingdom representative sides, and currently Northern Irish athletes compete for either [[Great Britain at the Olympics|Great Britain]] or [[Ireland at the Olympics|Ireland]].

Northern Ireland has competed in three [[FIFA World Cup]]s, reaching the quarter-final stage in the [[1958 FIFA World Cup|1958]] and [[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982]] tournaments. Northern Ireland held the accolade of being the smallest nation to qualify for a World Cup Finals from their first appearance in 1958 until 2006, when [[Trinidad and Tobago national football team|Trinidad and Tobago]] qualified for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]]. At [[UEFA Euro 2016]], the team made its first appearance at the European tournament and reached the round of 16. Northern Ireland last qualified for the World Cup in [[1986 FIFA World Cup|1986]].

== History ==
{{Main|Ireland national football team (1882–1950)}}

On 18 February 1882, 15 months after the founding of the Irish FA, Ireland made their international debut against [[England national football team|England]], losing 13–0 in a friendly played at Bloomfield in [[Belfast]]. This remains the record defeat for the team, and also England's largest winning margin. On 25 February 1882, Ireland played their second international, against [[Wales national football team|Wales]] at the [[Racecourse Ground]], Wrexham, and an equaliser from Johnston became Ireland's first ever goal.

In 1884, Ireland competed in the inaugural [[British Home Championship]] and lost all three games. Ireland did not win their first game until 19 February 1887, a 4–1 win over Wales in Belfast. Between their debut and this game, they had a run of 14 defeats and 1 draw, the longest run without a win in the 1800s. Despite the end of this run, heavy defeats continued. On 3 March 1888, they lost 11–0 to Wales and three weeks later, on 24 March, lost 10–2 to [[Scotland national football team|Scotland]]. Further heavy defeats came on 15 March 1890 when they lost 9–1 to England, on 18 February 1899 when they lost 13–2 to England and on 2 February 1901 when they lost 11–0 to Scotland.

In 1899, the Irish FA also changed its rules governing the selection of non-resident players. Before then the Ireland team selected its players exclusively from the Irish League, in particular the three Belfast-based clubs [[Linfield F.C.|Linfield]], [[Cliftonville F.C.|Cliftonville]] and [[Lisburn Distillery F.C.|Distillery]]. On 4 March 1899, for the match against Wales, McAteer included four Irish players based in England. The change in policy produced dividends as Ireland won 1–0. Three weeks later, on 25 March, one of these four players, [[Archie Goodall]], aged 34 years and 279 days, became the oldest player to score in international football during the 19th century when he scored Ireland's goal in a 9–1 defeat to Scotland.

In 1920, [[Partition of Ireland|Ireland was partitioned]] into [[Northern Ireland]] and [[Southern Ireland (1921–22)|Southern Ireland]]. In 1922, Southern Ireland gained independence as the [[Irish Free State]], later to become a republic under the name of [[Republic of Ireland|Ireland]]. Amid these political upheavals, a rival football association, the [[Football Association of Ireland]], emerged in [[Dublin]] in 1921 and organised a separate league and international team. In 1923, at a time when the home nations had withdrawn from [[FIFA]], the FAI was recognised by FIFA as the governing body of the Irish Free State on the condition that it changed its name to the Football Association of the Irish Free State. The Irish FA continued to organise its national team on an all-Ireland basis.

Between 1928 and 1946, the IFA were not affiliated to FIFA and the two Ireland teams co-existed, never competing in the same competition. On 8 March 1950, however, in a 0–0 draw with Wales at the Racecourse Ground in a [[FIFA World Cup]] [[1950 FIFA World Cup qualification|qualifier]], the IFA fielded a team that included four players who were born in the Irish Free State. All four players had previously played for the FAI in their qualifiers and as a result had played for two different associations in the [[1950 FIFA World Cup|same FIFA World Cup tournament]].

After complaints from the FAI, FIFA intervened and restricted players' eligibility based on the political border. In 1953 FIFA ruled neither team could be referred to as Ireland, decreeing that the FAI team be officially designated as the Republic of Ireland, while the IFA team was to become Northern Ireland.

== Past performances ==
=== British Home Championship ===
Until the 1950s, the major competition for Northern Ireland/Ireland was the [[British Home Championship]]. The team won the competition eight times, taking the title outright on three occasions. They were the last winners of the now defunct competition held in [[1983–84 British Home Championship|1984]], and hence still are the British champions, and the trophy remains the property of the Irish FA.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/that-1980s-sports-blog/2022/jun/15/northern-ireland-last-british-home-championship-billy-bingham-world-cup|title=When Northern Ireland won the last ever British Home Championship|date=15 June 2022|work=Guardian|accessdate=15 June 2022|archive-date=15 June 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220615112713/https://www.theguardian.com/football/that-1980s-sports-blog/2022/jun/15/northern-ireland-last-british-home-championship-billy-bingham-world-cup|url-status=live}}</ref>

=== FIFA World Cup ===
[[File:Aankomst Noordierse elftal op Zestienhoven; trainer Blanchflower en George Best (r).jpg|thumb|[[Danny Blanchflower]] (''left'') captained Northern Ireland at the [[1958 FIFA World Cup]], while [[George Best]] (''right''), winner of the [[1968 Ballon d'Or]], never reached a major international tournament with the team]]
Northern Ireland's best World Cup performance was in their first appearance in the finals, the [[1958 FIFA World Cup|1958 World Cup]], where they reached the quarter-finals after beating [[Czechoslovakia national football team|Czechoslovakia]] 2–1 in the play-off. They were knocked out by [[French national football team|France]], losing 4–0. In the 1958 competition, Northern Ireland became the least populous country to have qualified for the World Cup, a record that stood until [[Trinidad and Tobago national football team|Trinidad and Tobago]] qualified for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]]. Northern Ireland remains, however, the least populous country to have qualified for more than one World Cup finals tournament, to win a World Cup finals match, and to have progressed from the first round of the World Cup finals.

Captain of the national side at the 1958 World Cup was [[Danny Blanchflower]], who also captained [[Tottenham Hotspur F.C.|Tottenham Hotspur]] in the English league and was twice [[FWA Player of the Year|footballer of the year]] in England. His younger brother [[Jackie Blanchflower|Jackie]] was also a key member of the national team, and won two league titles in England with [[Manchester United F.C.|Manchester United]], until his career was ended by injuries suffered in the [[Munich air disaster]] of February 1958.

Despite the presence of world class forward [[George Best]], another Manchester United player, for the 1960s and 1970s, Northern Ireland failed to qualify for any major tournaments.

Northern Ireland qualified for the [[1982 FIFA World Cup|1982 World Cup]]. Their opening game was against [[Yugoslavia national football team|Yugoslavia]] at [[La Romareda]] stadium in [[Zaragoza]]. It was the international debut of 17-year-old [[Norman Whiteside]], who became the youngest player ever in the World Cup finals, a record that still stands. The game finished goalless. Five days later, they drew 1–1 with [[Honduras national football team|Honduras]], which was a disappointment, and many believed had doomed Northern Ireland's chances of advancing in the competition.<ref>{{cite book|last=Whiteside|first=Norman|title=Determined|publisher=[[Headline Publishing Group]]|year=2007|isbn=978-0-7553-1598-7|page=129}}</ref> They needed a win against hosts [[Spain men's national football team|Spain]] in the third and final group game at the [[Mestalla Stadium]] in [[Valencia]]. They faced a partisan atmosphere with a mostly Spanish crowd and a [[Spanish language|Spanish]]-speaking referee in [[Héctor Ortiz (referee)|Héctor Ortiz]] who — according to Whiteside — was unwilling to punish dirty play from the Spanish players.<ref>{{cite book|last=Whiteside|first=Norman|title=Determined|publisher=[[Headline Publishing Group]]|year=2007|isbn=978-0-7553-1598-7|page=135}}</ref> A mistake from Spain goalkeeper [[Luis Arconada]], however, gifted [[Gerry Armstrong (footballer)|Gerry Armstrong]] the only goal of the game, and despite having [[Mal Donaghy]] sent off on 60 minutes, Northern Ireland went on to record a historic 1–0 win and top the first stage group.

A 2–2 draw with [[Austria national football team|Austria]] at the [[Vicente Calderón Stadium]] meant that a win against France would take them into the semi-finals, however, a French team inspired by [[Michel Platini]] won 4–1 and eliminated Northern Ireland from the competition.

They also [[1986 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 3|qualified]] for the [[1986 FIFA World Cup|1986 World Cup]] where they went out in the [[1986 FIFA World Cup Group D|Group stages]], drawing [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]] and losing to Spain and [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]].<ref>{{cite web|url= https://www.theguardian.com/football/that-1980s-sports-blog/2017/nov/09/northern-ireland-qualified-world-cup-1986|title=How Northern Ireland qualified for their last World Cup in 1986|date=9 November 2017|work=Guardian|accessdate=13 February 2024}}</ref> [[Billy Bingham]], a member of the 1958 squad, was manager for both of these tournaments. They have not qualified for any other World Cups since.

=== Recent history ===
[[File:NI murals NI football.jpg|thumb|200px|The Our Wee Country mural in east Belfast commemorating Northern Ireland beating England at home in 2005.]]
[[Lawrie Sanchez]] was appointed in January 2004 after a run of 13 games without a goal under the previous manager [[Sammy McIlroy]], which was a European record for any international team until [[San Marino national football team|San Marino]] went over 20 games without scoring between October 2008 and August 2012. That run ended after his first game in charge, a 1–4 loss to [[Norway national football team|Norway]] in a friendly in February 2004. The run of 16 games without a win ended after his second game, a 1–0 victory in a friendly over [[Estonia national football team|Estonia]], with a largely experimental side, in March 2004.

On 7 September 2005, Northern Ireland beat England 1–0 in a 2006 World Cup [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|qualifier]] at [[Windsor Park]]. [[David Healy (footballer)|David Healy]] scored the winner in the 73rd minute. Almost a year later, on 6 September 2006, Northern Ireland defeated Spain 3–2 in a [[UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying Group F|qualifier]] for [[UEFA Euro 2008]], with Healy scoring a hat-trick. In June 2007, [[Nigel Worthington]] was named manager in the place of Lawrie Sanchez, who took over at [[Fulham F.C.|Fulham]]. Initially, Worthington took over until the end of the Euro 2008 qualifiers, but was later given a contract until the end of the [[UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying|Euro 2012 qualifiers]]. [[Michael O'Neill (footballer)|Michael O'Neill]] became manager in February 2012 after Worthington had resigned in October 2011 after a poor Euro 2012 qualification campaign.

The Northern Ireland team [[UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying Group F|qualified]] for its first ever UEFA European Championship, [[UEFA Euro 2016|Euro 2016]] in France after 30 years without qualifying for a major tournament, after beating [[Greece national football team|Greece]] 3–1 at Windsor Park on 8 October 2015.<ref>{{cite web|quote=At the tournament, the Northern Ireland fans made the famous chant ‘Will Grigg’s on fire’ famous|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/0/football/33543829|title=Northern Ireland 3 Greece 1|date=8 October 2015|work=BBC Sport|access-date=28 October 2015|archive-date=11 October 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151011235744/http://www.bbc.com/sport/0/football/33543829|url-status=live}}</ref> At the tournament, Northern Ireland were beaten 1–0 by [[Poland national football team|Poland]] on 20 June 2016 followed by a 2–0 win against [[Ukraine national football team|Ukraine]] on 16 June 2016 and finally a 1–0 loss against [[Germany national football team|Germany]] in the group stage. That was enough to qualify for a Round of 16 spot where they lost 1–0 to [[Wales national football team|Wales]] due to an unfortunate own goal by Gareth McAuley.<ref name="Jackson">{{cite news | url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/36486427 | title=Ukraine 0, Northern Ireland 2 | work=BBC Sport | date=16 June 2016 | access-date=16 June 2016 | author=Jackson, Lyle | archive-date=16 June 2016 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160616150740/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/36486427 | url-status=live }}</ref>

== Stadium ==
{{Main|Windsor Park}}

[[File:Windsor Park football stadium - Empty.JPG|thumb|Windsor Park before the 2015 redevelopment&nbsp;– a view from the Kop Stand, showing the two-tiered North Stand and the low Railway stand behind the opposite goal]]
Northern Ireland play their home matches at the Clearer Twist National Stadium at Windsor Park, [[Belfast]], home of [[Linfield F.C.|Linfield]], which they have use of on a 108-year lease, giving the owners 15% of revenue, including gate receipts and TV rights.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/irish/6902212.stm | work=BBC News | title=IFA wants out of Windsor contract | date=17 July 2007 | access-date=20 May 2010 | archive-date=15 August 2017 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170815142156/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/irish/6902212.stm | url-status=live }}</ref>

There was a proposal to build a multisports stadium for Northern Ireland at the disused [[Maze (HM Prison)|Maze prison]] outside [[Lisburn]] for the use of [[Rugby Union|Rugby]], [[Gaelic games]] and football.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld199697/ldhansrd/pdvn/lds06/text/60131w04.htm|title=Lord's Hansard on the question of building an NI national stadium|access-date=6 September 2017|archive-date=16 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181216163419/https://publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld199697/ldhansrd/pdvn/lds06/text/60131w04.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> This plan was given an "in principle" go-ahead by the Irish Football Association. However, it was opposed by fans, over 85% of whom in a match day poll conducted by the Amalgamation of Northern Ireland Supporters' Clubs ("AONISC") preferred to stay at a smaller new or redeveloped ground in the city of Belfast.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://stadiumforbelfast.com/news/maze-tide-turning.shtml|title=Tide Turns Against The Maze|access-date=20 July 2007|archive-date=17 August 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070817165242/http://stadiumforbelfast.com/news/maze-tide-turning.shtml|url-status=dead}}</ref> The AONISC organised a protest against the move to the Maze at the game against [[Estonia national football team|Estonia]] in March 2006.

The issue assumed ever greater urgency by 2007, following a series of inspections which questioned the suitability of Windsor Park to host international football.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/irish/6291836.stm Report slams Windsor Park safety]  BBC News</ref> Following a reduction of capacity due to the closure of the Railway Stand, the IFA made it known that they wished to terminate their contract for the use of the stadium.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/irish/6902212.stm IFA wants out of Windsor contract]  BBC News</ref> A report on health and safety in October 2007 indicated that the South Stand might have to be closed for internationals, which would further reduce the stadium's capacity to 9,000.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/northern_ireland/7032434.stm South Stand future under threat]  BBC News</ref>
In April 2008, Belfast City Council announced that they had commissioned Drivers Jonas to conduct a feasibility study into the building of a Sports Stadium in Belfast which could accommodate international football, which was followed at the beginning of May 2008 by speculation that the Maze Stadium project was going to be radically revised by [[Peter Robinson (Northern Ireland politician)|Peter Robinson]], the finance and personnel minister in the [[Northern Ireland Assembly]], so that any construction might be used for purposes other than football, rugby union and Gaelic games.
Given the time that is needed to build a new stadium, in the absence of significant work improving Windsor Park, it seemed to be likely that Northern Ireland might be forced to play their home games at a venue outside Northern Ireland for a period.

In March 2009, proposals were announced for the construction of a new 25,000-seat stadium in the [[Sydenham, Belfast|Sydenham]] area of East Belfast as an alternative to the Maze proposal. This would form part of a major development, with links to both [[George Best Belfast City Airport]] and the [[Belfast-Bangor railway line|Bangor railway line]]. The development would also include a hotel, and retail/leisure areas. The stadium itself would be used for both football and [[rugby union]], with [[Glentoran F.C.|Glentoran]] and [[Ulster Rugby]] intended as tenants. [[Ulster GAA]], however, who were a partner in the Maze proposal, stated that in the event of a new stadium being built in East Belfast, which is a major [[Unionism in Ireland|unionist]] area, their preference would then be to remain at [[Casement Park]] in [[Irish nationalism|nationalist]] west [[Belfast]].<ref>[https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/football/news-and-comment/plans-for-pound128m-belfast-stadium-unveiled-1653849.html Plans for £128m Belfast stadium unveiled] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180822113527/https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/football/news-and-comment/plans-for-pound128m-belfast-stadium-unveiled-1653849.html |date=22 August 2018 }}&nbsp;– The Independent, 25/03/09</ref>
[[File:Windsor Park redevelopment .jpg|alt=Internal view of Windsor park as the redevelopment nears completion.|thumb|336x336px|The new redeveloped Windsor Park. View from the Kop (West Stand) with only the corner between the West & North stands yet to be completed.]]
The IFA were initially non-committal about any of the proposals for improving their facilities, be it rebuilding Windsor Park, or supporting either the Maze or Sydenham proposals. In September 2009, however, they issued an announcement in favour of the redevelopment of Windsor Park.<ref>[http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/northern_ireland/8243020.stm IFA 'backs Windsor as NI stadium'] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170815141939/http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/northern_ireland/8243020.stm |date=15 August 2017 }}&nbsp;– BBC News, 07/09/09</ref> Although there were no specifics to this, Linfield had previously released a study with two proposals, of which the major one would be a £20&nbsp;million rebuilding of the stadium, raising the spectator capacity to 20,000.<ref>[http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/northern_ireland/8098028.stm Linfield FC has £20m stadium plan] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170815140829/http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/northern_ireland/8098028.stm |date=15 August 2017 }}&nbsp;– BBC News, 12/06/09</ref> In 2011, the [[Northern Ireland Executive]] allocated £138&nbsp;million for a major programme of stadium redevelopment throughout Northern Ireland, with £28&nbsp;million allocated to the redevelopment of Windsor Park.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.belfasttelegraph.co.uk/sport/football/local/stadiums-fit-for-our-heroes-on-way-at-last-15110370.html |title=Stadiums fit for our heroes on way at last Belfast Telegraph |date=11 March 2011 |access-date=8 May 2013 |publisher=[[Belfast Telegraph]] |archive-date=19 October 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121019193448/http://www.belfasttelegraph.co.uk/sport/football/local/stadiums-fit-for-our-heroes-on-way-at-last-15110370.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In June 2012, further details of the stadium's redevelopment were released. The plan was to redevelop Windsor Park into an 18,000 all-seater stadium with a series of phased works originally intended to begin in the summer of 2013. The redevelopment would include the demolition of the existing East and South Stand structures, to be replaced by new purpose built stands that would partially enclose the stadium; complete renovation of the existing North and West Stands; and construction of both new conferencing facilities and a new headquarters facility for the IFA.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.irishfa.com/news/item/7418/windsor-park-redevelopment-project/ |title=WINDSOR PARK REDEVELOPMENT PROJECT |access-date=8 May 2013 |date=25 June 2012 |publisher=[[Irish Football Association]] |archive-date=29 May 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130529161215/http://www.irishfa.com/news/item/7418/windsor-park-redevelopment-project/ |url-status=dead }}</ref>

In February 2013, planning permission for the redevelopment was granted. The cost of the project was estimated to be around £29.2&nbsp;million, of which £25.2&nbsp;million would come from government funding. It was initially planned for the work to begin in September 2013.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.irishfa.com/domestic/item/7995/green-light-for-stadium-redevelopment/ |title=GREEN LIGHT FOR STADIUM REDEVELOPMENT |access-date=8 May 2013 |date=20 February 2013 |publisher=[[Irish Football Association]] |archive-date=20 December 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131220234930/http://www.irishfa.com/domestic/item/7995/green-light-for-stadium-redevelopment/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> Two months later, however, Irish Premiership club [[Crusaders F.C.|Crusaders]] began legal proceedings to have the process judicially reviewed. As owners of the site, rivals Linfield were in line to receive not only a redeveloped stadium, but also £200,000 per annum from the IFA in land rent instead of the existing agreement which entitled Linfield to 15% of match revenue. Crusaders believed this to be against [[European Union]] competition law as well as a form of [[State aid (European Union)|state aid]] towards Linfield.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/22190984 |title=Windsor Park funding faces legal challenge from Crusaders |access-date=8 May 2013 |date=17 April 2013 |publisher=[[BBC Sport]] |archive-date=20 April 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130420040000/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/football/22190984 |url-status=live }}</ref> In a hearing that took place on 22 May 2013, Crusaders' request was granted. It was ruled that it was a possibility for the redevelopment to be classed as state aid towards Linfield. The aspect of the challenge concerning competition law, however, was dismissed.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/22625278 |title=Crusaders win right to oppose government funding for Windsor |access-date=23 May 2013 |date=22 May 2013 |publisher=[[BBC Sport]] |archive-date=13 June 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130613015621/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/football/22625278 |url-status=live }}</ref>

[[File:Northern Ireland national football team in 2021.jpg|thumb|Northern Ireland team in 2021]]
In July 2013, Crusaders agreed to a possible settlement brought forward by the judicial review. The details of the settlement were not made public, but Crusaders said that it had the "potential to benefit the entirety of the football family".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/23325488 |title=Crusaders support settlement on Windsor Park upgrade |access-date=23 July 2013 |date=16 July 2013 |publisher=[[BBC Sport]] |archive-date=20 July 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130720034129/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/football/23325488 |url-status=live }}</ref> In September 2013, sports minister [[Carál Ní Chuilín]] said that she was still committed to making sure the redevelopment went ahead as scheduled, after previously stating that she would not sign off on the funding until the IFA resolved "governance issues" surrounding David Martin's return to the role of deputy president.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/24288144 |title=Sports Minister Caral Ni Chuilin 'committed' to Windsor upgrade |access-date=20 December 2013 |date=26 September 2013 |publisher=[[BBC Sport]] |archive-date=28 September 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130928083518/http://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/0/football/24288144 |url-status=live }}</ref> In December 2013, three months after the work was originally scheduled to begin, the redevelopment was finally given the green light. The sports minister signed off on £31&nbsp;million to complete the project. The redevelopment finally got under way on 6 May 2014 after the 2013–14 domestic season had finished, eight months later than originally planned. The work was completed in 2015.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.irishfa.com/news/item/9062/work-begins-at-windsor-park/ |title=WORK BEGINS AT WINDSOR PARK |access-date=16 May 2014 |date=6 May 2014 |publisher=[[Irish Football Association]] |archive-date=17 May 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140517120400/http://www.irishfa.com/news/item/9062/work-begins-at-windsor-park/ |url-status=dead }}</ref>

== Team image ==
=== Colours ===
{{Commons|Northern Ireland national football team kits}}

Northern Ireland football team's traditional colours are green and white. Green has long been a traditional symbolic colour for Ireland.  The kit has been manufactured by Adidas since 2012. Prior to this, the kit was manufactured by Umbro.

=== Kit suppliers ===
{|class="wikitable"
|-
! style="background-color:#008000; color:white; {{box-shadow border|a|#000080|2px}}" |Kit provider
! style="background-color:#008000; color:white; {{box-shadow border|a|#000080|2px}}" |Period
|-
| [[File:Umbro logo (current).svg|30px|left]] {{flagicon|ENG}} [[Umbro]]
| 1975–1977
|-
| [[File:Original Adidas logo.svg|30px|left]] {{flagicon|FRG}} [[Adidas]]
| 1977–1990
|-
| [[File:Umbro logo (current).svg|30px|left]] {{flagicon|ENG}} [[Umbro]]
| 1990–1994
|-
| [[File:Asics Logo.svg|30px|left]] {{flagicon|JPN}} [[Asics]]
| 1994–1998
|-
| [[File:Olympics Sportswear.jpg|left|30x30px]] {{flagicon|BEL}} Olympic Sportswear
| 1998–1999
|-
| [[File:Patrick-logo.png|left|30x30px]] {{flagicon|BEL}} [[Patrick (shoe company)|Patrick]]
| 1999–2004
|-
| [[File:Umbro logo (current).svg|30px|left]] {{flagicon|ENG}} [[Umbro]]
| 2004–2012
|-
| [[File:Adidas isologo.svg|left|30x30px]]{{flagicon|GER}} [[Adidas]]
| 2012–
|}

=== Supporters ===
[[File:Green and White Army (Windsor Park, Belfast).jpg|thumb|right|The Green and White Army]]
{{Main|Green and White Army}}

The [[Green and White Army]] is the name given to the fans that follow the Northern Ireland national football team.

Since the defeat of England in 2005, there has been an increased demand for tickets exceeding supply.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/internationals/4880528.stm | title=BBC news story on NI ticket sales | date=5 April 2006 | access-date=6 January 2010 | work=BBC News | archive-date=24 December 2006 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061224032222/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/internationals/4880528.stm | url-status=live }}</ref> Tongue-in-cheek songs such as "We're not [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]], we're Northern Ireland" (sung to the tune of [[Battle Hymn of the Republic]], an [[American Civil War]] song), "It's Just Like Watching Brazil" and "Stand up for the Ulstermen" are popular at home matches.

One of the first footballing celebrities was former [[Manchester United F.C.|Manchester United]] and Northern Ireland footballer [[George Best]]. The 1968 [[Ballon d'Or|European Footballer of the Year]], Best won 37 caps and scored 9 goals for his country.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2005/nov/27/sport.comment1 | location=London | work=The Guardian | title=Football: George Best: Football's first icon | date=27 November 2005 | access-date=12 December 2016 | archive-date=2 February 2017 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170202140143/https://www.theguardian.com/football/2005/nov/27/sport.comment1 | url-status=live }}</ref>

Leading up to the [[UEFA Euro 2016|Euro 2016]], [[YouTuber]] Sean Kennedy released the song "Will Grigg's on Fire", a parody about Northern Irish national [[Will Grigg]] to the tune of "[[Freed From Desire]]" by Gala. The song became a popular chant and internet sensation. A studio version was released by London-based production duo [[Blonde (duo)|Blonde]], going on to reach number seven in the iTunes UK Top 100.<ref>{{cite news |title=Will Grigg's on fire: Parody song reaches number seven in ITunes UK Top 100 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-northern-ireland-36436180 |access-date=14 September 2019 |work=BBC |date=2 June 2016 |archive-date=3 July 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190703210455/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-northern-ireland-36436180 |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Historic controversy over sectarianism ===
[[File:Neil lennon and excalibur.jpg|thumb|Former captain [[Neil Lennon]] retired from international football due to sectarian death threats]]
A small element of Northern Ireland's support was, in the past, regarded as sectarian.<ref name="mcnal2010">{{cite web|author=Brian McNally|date=5 March 2010|title=Why Northern Ireland continue to pay the price for abuse dished out to Neil Lennon|url=http://www.mirrorfootball.co.uk/opinion/columnists/brian-mcnally/Why-the-talent-drain-to-the-Republic-shows-Northern-Ireland-are-still-paying-the-price-for-abuse-dished-out-to-Neil-Lennon-The-Brian-McNally-Column-article344981.html|access-date=21 February 2011|publisher=Daily Mirror|archive-date=16 May 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100516021929/http://www.mirrorfootball.co.uk/opinion/columnists/brian-mcnally/Why-the-talent-drain-to-the-Republic-shows-Northern-Ireland-are-still-paying-the-price-for-abuse-dished-out-to-Neil-Lennon-The-Brian-McNally-Column-article344981.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 2000 the IFA launched the "Football For All" campaign to tackle sectarianism and racism at Northern Ireland games. Michael Boyd, the director of football development at the IFA, recalled how the team struggled to even get a sponsor for their [[Kit (association football)|kit]] because the image of Northern Irish football was so poor.<ref name="sky20201">{{cite web|last=Gilmour|first=Paul|title=Neil Lennon threat 'a turning point' for Northern Ireland campaign|work=Sky Sports |accessdate=25 November 2022|url=https://www.skysports.com/football/news/12019/12135441/neil-lennon-threat-a-turning-point-for-northern-ireland-campaignaa|archive-date=25 November 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221125151200/https://www.skysports.com/football/news/12019/12135441/neil-lennon-threat-a-turning-point-for-northern-ireland-campaignaa|url-status=live}}</ref> Attendance at matches was low; at a 1999 game against France, then reigning [[1998 FIFA World Cup|World Cup]] champions, IFA could not sell out the ground and there were problems with "sectarian singing and racist abuse."<ref name="sky20201"/> [[Neil Lennon]], a Roman Catholic [[Celtic F.C.|Celtic]] player who had been subject to sectarian abuse from Northern Ireland fans while playing for Northern Ireland in Windsor Park, was issued a death threat by Loyalists and retired from international football in 2002 as a result.<ref>{{cite news|author=Tim Rich|date=23 August 2002|title=Death threat forces Lennon to place family feelings first|work=The Independent|location=London|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/home-news/death-threat-forces-lennon-to-place-family-feelings-first-640706.html|access-date=21 February 2011}}{{dead link|date=August 2021|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> Former [[Northern Ireland Office|Security Minister]] [[Jane Kennedy (politician)|Jane Kennedy]] said the threat against Lennon 'disgraced Northern Ireland in the eyes of the world' and widened the divide between the association and Catholic players and supporters.<ref name="sky20201"/> However, the incident represented a turning point in the campaign against sectarianism in the IFA which had seen slow progress in the previous two years.<ref name="sky20201"/>

Steps taken to eradicate the sectarian element within the support have been successful.<ref>{{cite news|date=30 October 2003|title=BBC News Star helps in graffiti removal|url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/northern_ireland/3228195.stm|access-date=6 January 2010|archive-date=3 July 2004|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040703161640/http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/northern_ireland/3228195.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> Lennon has been quick to praise these initiatives.<ref>{{cite news|date=25 October 2006|title=Lennon hails anti-sectarian drive|work=BBC News|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/irish/6085396.stm|access-date=6 January 2010|archive-date=8 November 2006|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061108164909/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/irish/6085396.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> He also praised the "Football For All" Outstanding Achievement Award Winner Stewart MacAfee<ref>{{cite web|title=Praise for IFA's Football for All Awards Night |date=Feb 25, 2007 |website=Irish Football Association |url=http://www.irishfa.com/the-ifa/news/2813/praise-for-ifas-football-for-all-awards-night|access-date=28 March 2007|archive-date=7 March 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070307233008/http://www.irishfa.com/the-ifa/news/2813/praise-for-ifas-football-for-all-awards-night/|url-status=dead }}</ref> for the work he has done to create a more inclusive atmosphere at international games.

{{Cquote|People like Stewart are the unsung heroes who have been brave enough to challenge sectarianism and who have actively created a more fun, safe and family-orientated atmosphere at international games. Fans like Stewart have made the atmosphere at Northern Ireland football games in recent years the envy of Fans across not only Europe but World football. From a personal point of view I would like to thank them for their efforts.|20px|20px|[[Neil Lennon]]
}}

In 2006, Northern Ireland's supporters were awarded the Brussels International Supporters Award<ref>{{cite web|date=24 August 2006|title=Northern Ireland Fans Are Officially The Best In Europe|url=http://www.irishfa.com/news/item/2390/northern-ireland-fans-are-officially-the-best-in-europe/|access-date=5 November 2012|publisher=Irishfa.com|archive-date=19 February 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110219015610/http://www.irishfa.com/news/item/2390/northern-ireland-fans-are-officially-the-best-in-europe/|url-status=dead}}</ref> for their charity work, general good humour and behaviour and efforts to stamp out sectarianism. Representatives of the Amalgamation of Official Northern Ireland Supporters' Clubs received the award from UEFA and EU representatives prior to the Northern Ireland–Spain game at Windsor Park in September 2006.

Northern Ireland [[Minister of Culture, Arts and Leisure]], [[Carál Ní Chuilín]], the first senior [[Sinn Féin]] representative to attend an international at Windsor Park, commended "the very real efforts that have been made by the IFA to tackle sectarianism at their matches" after a match in August 2011.<ref>{{cite news|date=11 August 2011|title=Caral Ni Chuilin attends NI game at Windsor Park|work=BBC News|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-northern-ireland-14466874|access-date=20 July 2018|archive-date=29 January 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190129004504/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-northern-ireland-14466874|url-status=live}}</ref> Twenty years after Lennon's resignation, Northern Ireland international games regularly sell out and women and children were "noticeable" amongst the spectators.<ref name="sky20201"/>

=== Media coverage ===
[[Premier Sports]] currently have the rights to show all of Northern Ireland's competitive international fixtures up until 2024.<ref>{{cite news|date=20 May 2022|title=Premier Sports agrees deal to broadcast Northern Ireland games from 2022-2024|work=Irish FA|url=https://www.irishfa.com/news/2022/may/premier-sports-agrees-deal-to-broadcast-northern-ireland-games-from-2022-2024|access-date=6 June 2022|archive-date=25 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220525224839/https://www.irishfa.com/news/2022/may/premier-sports-agrees-deal-to-broadcast-northern-ireland-games-from-2022-2024|url-status=live}}</ref>{{Update inline|date=June 2026}}

Highlights of qualifiers are shown on [[BBC Northern Ireland]] with rights to World Cup Finals and European Championships held jointly by [[BBC]] and [[ITV (TV network)|ITV]] - both channels shared coverage of Northern Ireland's games at [[Euro 2016]].

Dating from the 1960s, Northern Ireland's games were shown live on [[BBC Northern Ireland]], with highlights on network BBC via [[Sportsnight]] until the rights to home games were sold to Sky in 2007.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/internationals/6599507.stm |title=Sky TV deal to net IFA over £10m |work=BBC Sport |date=27 April 2007 |access-date=5 October 2013 |archive-date=25 August 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825021915/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/internationals/6599507.stm |url-status=live }}</ref> In May 2013, Sky acquired the rights to all Northern Ireland qualifying games for [[UEFA Euro 2016]] and the [[2018 FIFA World Cup]].<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.belfasttelegraph.co.uk/sport/football/international/northern-ireland-fans-can-reach-for-the-sky-again-with-new-tv-deal-29267857.html |first=Steven |last=Beacom |title=Northern Ireland fans can reach for the Sky again with new TV deal |work=Belfast Telegraph |publisher=INM |date=15 May 2013 |access-date=5 October 2013 |archive-date=7 October 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131007035846/http://www.belfasttelegraph.co.uk/sport/football/international/northern-ireland-fans-can-reach-for-the-sky-again-with-new-tv-deal-29267857.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
From 2008 to 2013, BBC Northern Ireland held the rights to highlights of all of Northern Ireland's home international qualifiers. But in May 2013, ITV secured a deal to show highlights of the European Qualifiers for [[Euro 2016]] and the [[2018 World Cup]], including Northern Ireland games, between 2014 and 2017.

In 2015, [[BBC Northern Ireland]] acquired the live rights to show Northern Ireland's friendlies in the run-up to [[UEFA Euro 2016]], but the next two subsequent home friendlies against Croatia and New Zealand were shown on [[Premier Sports]]/[[Eir Sport|eirSport]] until the contract ended before the [[2018 FIFA World Cup|2018 World Cup]].

== Results and fixtures ==
{{Main|Northern Ireland national football team results (2020–present)}}

The following is a list of match results in the last 12 months, as well as any future matches that have been scheduled.

== Players ==
{{Main|List of Northern Ireland international footballers}}

=== Current squad ===
The following players were called up for the [[Exhibition game#International football|friendly]] matches against [[Guinea national football team|Guinea]] and [[France national football team|France]] on 4 and 8 June 2026; respectively.<ref>{{cite news |title=Northern Ireland squad for June friendlies revealed |url=https://www.irishfa.com/news/2026/may/northern-ireland-squad-for-june-friendlies-revealed |access-date=27 May 2026 |publisher=Irish Football Association |date=27 May 2026}}</ref>

''Caps and goals updated as of 8 June 2026, after the match against [[France national football team|France]]''.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://eu-football.info/_players.php?id=148&data=9|title=Most Northern Ireland Caps - EU-Football.info|website=eu-football.info|access-date=9 June 2022|archive-date=23 July 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200723084129/https://eu-football.info/_players.php?id=148&data=9|url-status=live}}</ref>

=== Recent call-ups ===
The following players have been called up to the Northern Ireland squad during the last 12 months.

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[George Saville]]|age={{Birth date and age|1993|6|1|df=y}}|caps=64|goals=0|club=[[Luton Town F.C.|Luton Town]]|clubnat=ENG|latest=v. {{fb|WAL}}, 31 March 2026}}

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=[[Caolan Boyd-Munce]]|age={{Birth date and age|2000|1|26|df=y}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[Wycombe Wanderers F.C.|Wycombe Wanderers]]|clubnat=ENG|latest=v. {{fb|GER}}, 7 September 2025}}

{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Dion Charles]]|age={{Birth date and age|1995|10|7|df=y}}|caps=33|goals=4|club=[[Huddersfield Town A.F.C.|Huddersfield Town]]|clubnat=ENG|latest=v. {{fb|WAL}}, 31 March 2026}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Dale Taylor]]|age={{Birth date and age|2003|12|12|df=y}}|caps=9|goals=0|club=[[Blackpool F.C.|Blackpool]]|clubnat=ENG|latest=v. {{fb|GER}}, 7 September 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Ronan Hale]]|age={{Birth date and age|1998|9|8|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Gillingham F.C.|Gillingham]]|clubnat=ENG|latest=v. {{fb|GER}}, 7 September 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
<sup>INJ</sup> = Withdrew due to an injury.<br />
<sup>PRE</sup> = Preliminary squad / standby.<br />
<sup>RET</sup> = Retired from the national team.<br />
<sup>SUS</sup> = Serving suspension.<br />
<sup>WTD</sup> = Player withdrew from the squad due to non-injury issue.
{{nat fs end}}

== Individual records ==
{{Main|Northern Ireland national football team records and statistics}}

{{Updated|8 June 2026}} ''after the match against [[France national football team|France]].''
{{legend|#CCFFCC|Players still active are highlighted in green|border=#AAAAAA}}

===Most appearances===

[[File:Steven Davis, CZE-NIR 2019-10-14 (2).jpg|190px|thumb|right|Midfielder [[Steven Davis]] is Northern Ireland's most capped player with 140 appearances.]]

{|class="wikitable sortable" style="text-align:center;"

===Top goalscorers===

[[File:David Healy (footballer).jpg|170px|thumb|right|[[David Healy (footballer)|David Healy]] is Northern Ireland men team's all-time top goalscorer with 36 goals.]]

*<small>A goalkeeper is awarded a clean sheet if he does not concede a goal during his time on the pitch.</small>

*<small> Captaincy appearances are only awarded to players who were assigned the captaincy at the start of a game.</small>

== Competitive record ==
''For the all-time record of the national team against opposing nations, see the team's [[Northern Ireland national football team records and statistics#All-time records|all-time record page]].''

=== FIFA World Cup ===
{{Main|Northern Ireland at the FIFA World Cup}}

|[[Lawrie Sanchez]], [[Nigel Worthington]]{{refn|group=note|[[Lawrie Sanchez]] managed for the first six qualifying matches. [[Nigel Worthington]] managed the remainder of the qualification campaign.}}
|-

=== Summary of results ===
;All competitive matches<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.irishfa.com/international/fixtures-and-results/historical-results/ |title=Historical Results&nbsp;– Fixtures And Results&nbsp;– International&nbsp;– The Irish Football Association |publisher=Irishfa.com |date=16 July 2012 |access-date=5 November 2012 |archive-date=26 October 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121026124114/http://www.irishfa.com/international/fixtures-and-results/historical-results/ |url-status=dead }}</ref>

|-

|| 571 || 149 || 124 || 298 || 615 || 1078 || −463
|}

Belfast:Irish Football Association</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Jackson |first=Lyle |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/internationals/8360701.stm |title=BBC:''Northern Ireland 0–1 Serbia'' |publisher=BBC News |date=14 November 2009 |access-date=2 July 2010}}</ref><sup>‡</sup>

=== FIFA ranking history ===

== Honours ==
===Regional===
* '''[[British Home Championship]]'''
** '''Champions (8)''': [[1903 British Home Championship|1903]]{{sup|s}}*, [[1914 British Home Championship|1914]]*, [[1956 British Home Championship|1956]]{{sup|s}}, [[1958 British Home Championship|1958]]{{sup|s}}, [[1959 British Home Championship|1959]]{{sup|s}}, [[1964 British Home Championship|1964]]{{sup|s}}, [[1980 British Home Championship|1980]], [[1984 British Home Championship|1984]]

;Notes:
* {{sup|s}} Shared titles
* <small>*</small> Titles won as {{fb|IRE}}
